title: Watercolour journal page preparation, tipping in dedicated sheets
subtitle: How to add watercolour paper pages to a journal that wasn't designed for wet media
excerpt: Standard journal paper buckles and pills under watercolour washes. Tipping in dedicated watercolour paper pages at key points gives a journal the correct surface for wet media without replacing the whole book. This article covers the selection, cutting, and attachment of tipped-in watercolour sheets.

glossaryTerms.definition: A single sheet attached to a journal or book with a thin strip of adhesive along one edge only. The tipped-in sheet lies flat when the book is open but is not a structural part of the binding.
Watercolour paper with a medium-texture surface produced by pressing the sheet through unheated rollers. Cold press takes washes evenly, holds detail, and resists buckling better than lighter papers.

content.text: Most journals, even those marketed for mixed media, use paper that is too light for watercolour washes. Paper under 200 gsm buckles when wet and the surface pills if reworked.
watercolour paper at the relevant pages is a practical solution. The tip-in sheet behaves like a full journal page but can be painted heavily without affecting the surrounding pages.

items.name: Cold press watercolour paper, 300 gsm
items.notes: The standard weight for journal tipping. Lighter weights still buckle; heavier weights are unnecessarily thick. Cut from a full sheet to avoid buying full pads at a premium.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For scoring the attachment hinge precisely.

items.name: Craft knife and cutting mat
items.notes: For trimming the tip-in to journal page size.

items.name: Metal ruler, 30 cm
items.notes: For guiding all cuts and scores.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: Applied as a thin strip to the hinge edge only. Avoid spreading adhesive into the painting area.

content.text: Trim the watercolour sheet to the journal page height and 10-12 mm narrower than the journal page width. The narrower cut prevents the tip-in from protruding past the journal edge when closed. Score a light fold line 8 mm from one long edge using the bone folder against a ruler. Fold along this score: this 8 mm strip becomes the attachment hinge.

Apply a thin, even layer of PVA to the back of the 8 mm hinge strip. Position the tip-in against the journal page gutter, hinge face down, and press firmly. Lay the journal flat and weight it for 20 minutes with the tip-in page held open at 90 degrees. This prevents the hinge from sticking the tip-in flat to the page and ensures it will open fully when dry.

Work with the tip-in page fully open and the journal laid flat. For heavy washes, slip a piece of scrap card under the tip-in to prevent wet paint bleeding through to the page below. Avoid painting over the hinge area. Once dry, the tip-in folds back flat into the journal. If the sheet has buckled after drying, press it under a weight overnight with the journal closed.
